content:
# Matthew Chapter 20 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_020_page_001.jpg

### The Parable of the Vineyard Workers

1. "The kingdom of heaven is like a landowner who went out at dawn to hire workers for his vineyard.

2. He agreed to pay them the standard daily wage and sent them to work.

3. Around nine in the morning, he went out again and saw others standing around the marketplace with nothing to do.

4. 'You go work in my vineyard too,' he told them. 'I'll pay you a fair wage.' So they went.

5. He went out again around noon and at three in the afternoon and did the same thing.

6. Finally, around five o'clock, he went out and found still others standing around. 'Why have you been standing here doing nothing all day?' he asked.

7. 'Because nobody hired us,' they answered. 'Go work in my vineyard,' he told them.

8. That evening, the owner told his foreman, 'Call the workers and pay them, starting with the last ones hired and ending with the first.'

9. The workers hired at five o'clock came forward, and each received a full day's pay.

10. When the first workers came, they expected to receive more. But they too received a full day's pay.

11. They took it but complained to the landowner:

12. 'These people who were hired last only worked one hour, and you've paid them the same as us — we who worked all day in the blazing heat!'

13. He answered one of them, 'Friend, I haven't cheated you. Didn't you agree to work for the standard wage?

14. Take your pay and go. I want to give the last workers the same as I gave you.

15. Don't I have the right to do what I want with my own money? Or are you jealous because I'm generous?'

16. So those who are last will be first, and the first will be last."

### Jesus Predicts His Death a Third Time

17. As Jesus was heading up to Jerusalem, he pulled the twelve disciples aside and said to them privately,

18. "Listen, we're going up to Jerusalem, where the Son of Man will be handed over to the chief priests and teachers of the law. They will sentence him to death

19. and hand him over to the Romans to be mocked, flogged, and crucified. But on the third day he will be raised to life."

### A Mother's Request

20. Then the mother of James and John, the sons of Zebedee, came to Jesus with her sons and knelt before him to make a request.

21. "What do you want?" he asked. She said, "Promise me that in your kingdom, these two sons of mine will sit on thrones next to yours — one on your right and one on your left."

22. Jesus replied, "You don't realize what you're asking. Can you drink the cup of suffering I'm about to drink?" "Yes, we can!" they answered.

23. He said, "You will indeed drink my cup. But I have no say in who sits at my right or left. My Father has already decided that."

24. When the other ten disciples heard about this, they were furious with the two brothers.

25. Jesus called them all together and said, "You know how it works in the world — rulers dominate their subjects, and powerful people push everyone around.

26. That's not how it will be among you. Whoever wants to be great among you must become your servant.

27. Whoever wants to be first must become your slave.

28. The Son of Man didn't come to be served — he came to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many."

### Two Blind Men Healed

29. As they left Jericho, a large crowd followed them.

30. Two blind men were sitting by the roadside. When they heard that Jesus was passing by, they shouted, "Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!"

31. The crowd told them to be quiet, but they shouted even louder, "Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!"

32. Jesus stopped and called out to them. "What do you want me to do for you?"

33. "Lord," they said, "we want to see!"

34. Moved with compassion, Jesus touched their eyes. Instantly they could see, and they followed him.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-16 — The vineyard parable:** This story offends our sense of fairness — and that's exactly the point. The kingdom of heaven does not operate on the principle of merit. It operates on the principle of grace. The landowner is not unjust to the early workers (they got what was agreed). He is simply outrageously generous to the late ones. The grumbling reveals the real problem: not injustice, but jealousy at someone else's good fortune. Grace is only offensive when you think you've earned what you have.

**Verse 19 — "mocked, flogged, and crucified":** This is the third and most detailed passion prediction. For the first time, Jesus explicitly mentions crucifixion and identifies the Gentiles (Romans) as the executioners. The specificity is increasing as they approach Jerusalem.

**Verse 22 — "the cup":** In Old Testament prophecy, "the cup" is the cup of divine wrath and judgment (see Isaiah 51:17, Jeremiah 25:15-28). Jesus is asking: "Can you drink the cup of God's judgment that I will drink?" James and John blithely say yes. They have no idea what they're agreeing to. James will be the first apostle martyred (Acts 12:2).

**Verse 28 — "ransom for many":** This is Jesus' clearest statement of why he dies. His death is a λύτρον — a price paid to release captives. The "many" echoes Isaiah 53:11-12: the Servant who "bore the sin of many." Jesus interprets his death not as tragedy but as purpose.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
